old_claim.note: Banking (Early Forms) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: A charterparty is a contract between shipowner and charterer; contract law supplies the enforceable agreement structure.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica defines a charter party as a contract by which a shipowner lets a ship or principal part of it to another for carriage of goods on a voyage or for a specified time.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source defines charterparty as a ship-hire or cargo-capacity contract between shipowner and charterer.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing banking because the direct mechanism is enforceable contract formation, while banking is only adjacent trade finance context.

ontology_before: Charterparty Shipping Contracts depended on Banking, implying early finance was the direct predecessor for ship-hire contracts.
ontology_after: Charterparty Shipping Contracts depends on Contract Law for the agreement structure and Maritime Law Codes for the shipping legal domain.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: charterparty_shipping_contracts now depends on contract_law and no longer directly depends on banking_early_forms.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to maritime loans or bank-financed shipping ventures rather than charterparty ship-hire contracts.
A stronger source showed banking institutions were necessary before charterparty agreements could exist.
The contract_law node were narrowed away from enforceable commercial agreements.
short_reason: A charterparty is a contract; banking is adjacent finance context.
